name: Sinus Rhythm
description: Normal heart rhythm originating from the sinoatrial (SA) node, typically between 60–100 BPM with regular P-QRS-T cycles.
findings: Regular rhythm, rate 60–100 BPM, upright P waves in leads I and II, PR interval 0.12–0.20 s.
treatment: No treatment required. Maintain healthy lifestyle: regular exercise, balanced diet, stress management.

name: Sinus Bradycardia
description: Regular sinus rhythm with a rate below 60 BPM. May be normal in athletes or indicate underlying disease.
findings: Regular rhythm, rate < 60 BPM, normal P waves, normal PR and QRS intervals.
treatment: Asymptomatic: observation. Symptomatic: atropine 0.5–1 mg IV, or transcutaneous pacing. Chronic: permanent pacemaker if indicated.

name: Sinus Tachycardia
description: Rapid sinus rhythm over 100 BPM, usually a physiological response to stress, pain, fever, dehydration or exercise.
findings: Regular rhythm, rate > 100 BPM, normal P waves, PR interval may shorten slightly.
treatment: Treat underlying cause: hydration, fever control, pain management, anxiolytics. Beta-blockers if persistent.

name: Sinus Arrhythmia
description: Irregular variation of the sinus rhythm associated with respiratory cycles (rate increases on inspiration).
findings: P-P intervals vary > 0.12 s, otherwise normal morphology. Variation tracked to breathing.
treatment: No treatment necessary. It is a normal physiological variant in children and young adults.

name: Sinus Exit Block
description: Failure of the impulse generated by the SA node to conduct to the atria, producing a pause on the ECG.
findings: Sudden pause without a P wave; the pause is a multiple of the normal P-P interval (Type II).
treatment: Treat underlying cause (ischemia, electrolyte imbalance, drug toxicity). Permanent pacemaker if symptomatic.

name: Sinus Arrest
description: Temporary cessation of SA node activity causing a prolonged pause not related to a multiple of the P-P interval.
findings: Prolonged pause > 2 s, no P wave during pause. A junctional or ventricular escape beat may follow.
treatment: Atropine or epinephrine acutely. Permanent pacemaker implantation is usually required.

name: NSR with PAC
description: Normal sinus rhythm interrupted by early beats arising from an ectopic atrial focus (Premature Atrial Contractions).
findings: Premature, narrow QRS complexes with a preceding abnormal P wave; followed by non-compensatory pause.
treatment: Usually benign; reduce caffeine, alcohol, and stress. Beta-blockers if symptomatic.

name: SVT
description: Supraventricular Tachycardia: rapid heart rate originating above the ventricles, typically in the AV node.
findings: Abrupt onset, rate 150–250 BPM, narrow QRS (unless aberrant conduction), P waves may be hidden in QRS.
treatment: Valsalva maneuver / carotid massage. Adenosine 6 mg IV (first-line). Electrical cardioversion if unstable. Ablation for recurrent SVT.

name: Atrial Fibrillation
description: Most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia. Chaotic, disorganized atrial electrical activity causes irregular ventricular response.
findings: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline (f-waves), variable R-R intervals.
treatment: Rate control: beta-blockers or calcium-channel blockers. Rhythm control: antiarrhythmics. Anticoagulation for stroke prevention.

name: Atrial Flutter
description: Organized macro-reentrant atrial circuit producing rapid flutter waves at ~300 BPM with a characteristic 2:1 or 4:1 block.
findings: Sawtooth flutter waves (F-waves) at 250–350 BPM in leads II, III, aVF; regular ventricular rate.
treatment: Rate control same as AFib. Cardioversion. Catheter ablation of cavotricuspid isthmus is highly effective.

name: Paced Atrial
description: Rhythm maintained by an atrial pacemaker lead, producing a pacemaker spike before each P wave.
findings: Pacemaker spike before narrow QRS. Fixed or demand pacing depending on mode (AAI, DDD).
treatment: Ensure pacemaker is functioning properly. Regular device check-ups every 6–12 months.

name: NSR with 1° AVB
description: Constant delay in AV conduction reflected as a prolonged PR interval (>200 ms) on every beat.
findings: PR interval > 200 ms (0.20 s), otherwise normal P-QRS-T morphology. Every P conducted.
treatment: Usually benign, no specific treatment. Monitor for progression. Stop offending drugs (beta-blockers, digoxin).

name: 2° AVB Type I
description: Wenckebach block: progressive PR prolongation until a P wave is not conducted (dropped beat), then cycle repeats.
findings: Progressive PR lengthening, grouped beats, dropped QRS (non-conducted P). R-R intervals shorten before pause.
treatment: Usually benign; treat reversible causes. Pacing if symptomatic.

name: 2° AVB Type II
description: Mobitz II: intermittent dropped beats without prior PR prolongation. High risk of progression to complete block.
findings: Constant PR interval, then sudden non-conducted P wave; QRS may be wide (bundle branch block pattern).
treatment: Requires permanent pacemaker implantation even if asymptomatic.

name: 2° AVB 2:1
description: Every alternate P wave is blocked, resulting in a ventricular rate half the atrial rate.
findings: 2 P waves per QRS, regular rhythm, rate typically 35–55 BPM.
treatment: Permanent pacemaker required for symptomatic patients or distal block.

name: 3° AV Block
description: Complete AV dissociation. Atria and ventricles beat independently. Ventricular rate maintained by an escape rhythm.
findings: P waves and QRS complexes are independent. Ventricular rate 20–40 BPM. Wide QRS if escape is ventricular.
treatment: Emergency temporary pacing. Permanent pacemaker (Class I indication).

name: NSR with PJC
description: Normal sinus rhythm with premature beats arising from the AV junction (PJC = Premature Junctional Contractions).
findings: Early, narrow QRS; P wave may be absent, retrograde (negative in II), or follow the QRS.
treatment: Usually benign and asymptomatic. Beta-blockers if symptomatic.

name: Junctional Rhythm
description: Escape rhythm from the AV junction at 40–60 BPM, occurring when sinus node function fails.
findings: Rate 40–60 BPM, narrow QRS, absent or retrograde P waves, regular rhythm.
treatment: Treat underlying cause. Atropine or temporary pacing if symptomatic.

name: Accelerated Junctional
description: Enhanced AV junctional automaticity generating a rate of 60–100 BPM; often seen in digitalis toxicity or inferior MI.
findings: Rate 60–100 BPM, narrow QRS, absent or retrograde P waves.
treatment: Treat underlying cause (digitalis toxicity, inferior MI). Usually transient.

name: Junctional Tachycardia
description: Enhanced junctional automaticity with rates exceeding 100 BPM; may be confused with SVT.
findings: Rate > 100 BPM, narrow QRS, AV dissociation or retrograde P waves visible.
treatment: Treat cause. Beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers for rate control. Ablation in refractory cases.

name: Wandering Pacemaker
description: Pacemaker focus shifts between SA node, AV junction, and atrial sites, resulting in varying P-wave morphology.
findings: Variable P-wave morphology (≥3 different shapes), varying PR intervals, irregular rate.
treatment: Usually benign. Treat underlying conditions (COPD, digitalis toxicity) if identified.

name: NSR with PVC
description: Normal sinus rhythm with premature contractions from an ectopic ventricular focus (PVC = Premature Ventricular Contractions).
findings: Early, wide (>120 ms) and bizarre QRS without preceding P wave; followed by a full compensatory pause.
treatment: Asymptomatic: reassurance. Symptomatic: beta-blockers. Rule out structural disease. Ablation if frequent.

name: Idioventricular
description: Very slow ventricular escape rhythm (20–40 BPM), emerging when all higher pacemakers fail.
findings: Very wide QRS > 160 ms, rate 20–40 BPM, AV dissociation, no preceding P waves.
treatment: Emergent pacing. CPR if pulseless. Permanent pacemaker required.

name: Accelerated IVR
description: Accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR): ventricular focus 40–100 BPM, often seen as a reperfusion rhythm after MI.
findings: Wide QRS complex, rate 40–100 BPM, AV dissociation or fusion beats, regular rhythm.
treatment: Often self-limiting after reperfusion. Suppression rarely needed.

name: VTach
description: Three or more consecutive ventricular beats at ≥ 100 BPM. Life-threatening if sustained.
findings: Wide QRS > 120 ms, rate > 100 BPM, AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats; axis deviation.
treatment: Pulseless V-Tach: immediate defibrillation (200 J). Stable V-Tach: IV amiodarone. ICD for recurrent V-Tach.

name: VFib
description: Ventricular Fibrillation: completely disorganized ventricular electrical activity. No effective cardiac output.
findings: Chaotic, irregular, high-amplitude waveforms with no identifiable P, QRS, or T waves.
treatment: CPR immediately. Defibrillation (200 J biphasic). Epinephrine 1 mg IV. Amiodarone 300 mg. Treat reversible causes.

name: Paced Ventricular
description: Rhythm generated by a ventricular pacemaker lead, producing a pacemaker spike before each wide QRS complex.
findings: Pacemaker spike followed by wide, LBBB-pattern QRS complex. Fixed or demand pacing.
treatment: Regular pacemaker interrogation. Ensure appropriate sensing and capture thresholds.
